item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really hate to break this thing's, like, year-long 69-comment streak, but I've updated the image to one that's creative-commons compliant from here.
Thanks! I love you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The resulting explosion was explained to onlookers and the press as a GPS satellite"

This threw me, because up until this line I thought it was intentionally talking about Challenger. The photograph is reminiscent of Challenger, I presumed the incident 19XX- was 1986. The blackout of SCPS XXXXX Is about the right length for Challenger. I felt weird about Challenger being used in that way, for the usual not-using-real-life-disasters reasons.

But then suddenly the cover story is a GPS satellite.

And then the incident reports go in another direction entirely. A much better one than "Challenger was caused by a rogue foundation researcher", but all the old Challenger feels distracted me from the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is Major Tom to Ground Control. I'm feeling very scared.
Wait, who's telling them to never go to space?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me think of a dream I once had. It was an Earth where there was no knowledge of space at all. I believe the reason for this is because in the past of this parallel Earth, astronauts went to the other side of the moon and saw that something had eaten much of the moon so that it resembled an apple core, which was revealed to me later in the dream, and the sight terrified the world's inhabitants so much that all knowledge of space was destroyed so no one would ever encounter what did that to the moon. It had been so long not even anyone in the government knew anything about space. Somehow they made it so the moon and stars could not be seen… So that's interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good concept, however to make it more interesting, you should connect this SCP with the multiple conspiracies of the the possible murders of famous astronomers and inventors such as Tycho Brahe or Galileo. There are many who think that such individuals were about to stumble across something, an equation or a device, that would allow or accelerate the production and means for space travel among other technologies. Perhaps SCP-2001 has been around much longer than we thought? Merely a suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was one of my favorite SCPs when it was first written and reading it today (after finally making an account) I was actually pretty disappointed with the final addendum about alpha-prime. I really liked the idea of an SCP that seemed to be outright benevolent, with the Foundation fighting against it out of distrust and habit more than anything. It helps that at the time I first saw it I had just started reading those of H.P. Lovecraft's stories that aren't outright horror. It's still good writing and it gets my upvote, even if I intend it more for the November 2013 version than the ones that came after.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Funnier variation of "it's totally aliens" explanation would be that SCP-2001 is a containment measure that some extraterrestrial Foundation came up with when they encountered humanity and decided that this anomaly should be dealt with.)

Funny? Maybe not (see Incident 2631-1987A).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First impression after reading this was "well, of course the isn't any 'cuddly space grandmother' that wants to protect us, this is absolutely an alien plot to impair human technology and humanity's ability to bother extraterristrial spacefaring species". Which means that I read too much Eclipse Phase game material earlier this week.

What is good about this anomaly is sufficient amount of ambiguity. "Protective space caring of us" is just something the affected researcher experienced, nothing other in the article indicates that it is actual truth behind the anomaly. On other had it doesn't say that this is not the truth too. The ambiguity is here and the anomaly may be produced for some benign or nefarious purpose.

Which is good.

(Funnier variation of "it's totally aliens" explanation would be that SCP-2001 is a containment measure that some extraterrestrial Foundation came up with when they encountered humanity and decided that this anomaly should be dealt with.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It should be space odyssey, not oddity.

It's a pun.

To my understanding, its a disease

SCP-2001 is an anomalous series of neural oscillations

This is not a disease.

When you are writing, it is important to remember that the reader will not always be an MIT graduate who understands everything like REM sleep.

1): In-universe, most likely everybody reading this specific document will have education in this field.

2): I know nothing about sleeping science, and I understood this perfectly.

Also, the post your responding to is from 3 years ago, and the user hasn't been active since 2015.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. It should be space odyssey, not oddity.
2. What even is this? To my understanding, its a disease that makes people either dislike space, make conspiracy theories about space and become paranoid, or hurt people involved with space travel. Its not really a good concept in my opinion.

Also, I appreciate that you were clinical, but this was clinical to the point I cant understand. This could just be me, but this is just my opinion. When you are writing, it is important to remember that the reader will not always be an MIT graduate who understands everything like REM sleep.

I just wanted to get my opinion out. Maybe to other people this is good. I dont feel it though.

Have nice day, friend. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just got the name. Oh jeez I am dumb

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So between this and 1100, apparently humanity has an abusive mother and a doting grandmother.

Also, hello Pattern Screamer, I see you hiding there in the second addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the basic idea of "space is trying to protect us from exploring it", but the article as a whole didn't work for me. Keeping the "alpha prime" version secret seemed forced and unnecessary; it felt like you were trying too hard to make sure there was a "twist". I echo some of the other comments with being a bit uncomfortable about blaming "i just don't like space exploration" on an scp. I feel like it would be better if you cut the article to just be about alpha and/or alpha-prime. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This scared me. That last portion was really well done. I love space travel I love seeing what's out there. But seeing what this does to it legitimately scares me, I know it's fiction but I'm afraid this'll happen, like wow.
Maybe it's just me.

oh yeah +1 to oblivion and back, then back to oblivion

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only real problem with this article is the lack of major threat. I was expecting something with the mind being affected to feel more ominous and threatening. You definitely have the first part down, but maybe too ominous. I really don't quite know what is going on with this SCP beyond making people hate space exploration. You could definitely add more to make it feel like a bigger threat to the space industry. If you're interested, I could give you some ideas and see if you like them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The cosmos as a mother hen? Hah, yeah, I'm taking Jr. Researcher H. M.'s words with a grain of salt. It seems more like a subtle brainwashing campaign undertaken by an alien species to me.

However, I don't know, something in this article bothers me, but I can't place a finger on what.
No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Read the name. Groaned. Had to upvote. Take the 1+.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's definitely my favourite thing about this SCP. It could be honest or misleading, helpful or malicious, recent or ancient, and it would still be a plausible and cool concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thought ran through my head too. It wouldn't make sense to me to put phrases that potentially activate the effect into the security for the article, but I might be missing something. Alternatively, maybe the phrases are pretty common inspirational sayings about space- the kind of thing that might be passed around just before a launch, for instance. Just thinking…

Anyways, I like this article! It's fairly standard memetic stuff but that undercurrent of "shit, let's stay out of space" just, I dunno, does it for me. A ring of existential fear around it all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For some reason I really like the idea that something or someone out there really doesn't want us to go out into space. It falls right between something understandable and common (a "stay out of our territory" message) and something inhuman and arbitrary, which is the place I think SCPs should aim for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "security memetics" are actually the trigger phrases, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A tad too cliched for me but I like the language that is used as it is mostly accurate. Upvote all the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Are they trying to protect us from the terrible secret of space?

Anyway, the nebulous science of this is what makes it work for me. On one hand, you can easily take it at face value. "Lolcrazyantispacepeople"

Or you can ask… Is the foundation looking in the wrong place? Is.it using a convenient explanation instead of wondering if these people are RIGHT?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That actually is the case. Once it happens once, you're a subject.

In that case:

1) How did the Foundation ever figure it out? Were they doing some sleep experiments for something else entirely, and the subjects just coincidentally got affected and they woke up being anti-space?

2) How can the Foundation tell the difference between someone who is normally anti-space versus a victim of the SCP?

item.title: +1 for anti-eldritch bigotry
item.content:encoded:
This reminds me of a randomly horrifying punchline from The Adventures of Dr. McNinja.

Not attracting the attention of the giant space monsters seems like a good, hope-inducing plan

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1: Thanks! Didn't know that. Will change.
2: That actually is the case. Once it happens once, you're a subject.
3: Also a good point. I'll correct this.
4: Will look those up and stuff. They sound interesting.
Thanks a ton for the feedback! I'll correct these once I find my computer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) Terminology: your use of the word "carrier" is wrong. If John Doe is a carrier of X, that mean's he's not affected by X, but is capable of spreading X to others. Use something like "subject" or "instance" instead.

2) "At the conclusion of SCP-2001's active state, the subject is conditioned" makes it sound like the abnormal neural activity happens only once.

3) The article keeps switching between "manned spaceflight" and "space exploration", which aren't the same thing; the article should stick to one, or explicitly say that they're opposed to both. And if they're opposed to unmanned space exploration, you might want to make it explicit that they aren't opposed to things like communications and weather satellites. Or if some/all of them are opposed to satellites (I'm not sure if launching satellites is included under "space travel") then make that explicit, and also make clear if they're just opposed to launching new satellites, or if they also want to get rid of existing satellites.

4) You might want to note whether or not they're opposed to SETI and the Arecibo message.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You could theoretically still move it to another slot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed it.
EDIT: double-fixed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh shoot, I didn't even think to look there! I'm really sorry! I just thought we would all do it independently and had no idea there was even a thread for that. My bad!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am withholding my upvote until you rename it "SCP-2001: A Space Odyssey".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was me. It's over and done, contest rules said "first come, first serve." He came first, it's served. I'm not thrilled, but I'll live with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it was Crayne I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know, we had a thread in the contest hub page where we were claiming numbers that we wanted, and someone had already requested this one first. Rude. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. Neutral for the contest, but I like it.
It does feel a little unfocused with 4 different kinds of condition, and so much of it out there feels close to an explanation for all people who fear spaceflight.
One thing that is absent is a questioning of the originator's intentions. Supposing it actually is "Space" (because nobody who sends out potent meme's every lies…), is it doing this because it actually loves us? Perhaps it doesn't like us ejecting a bunch of junk into it's uniform void. Perhaps it hates us and wants to keep us contained until something else can finish us off. There's lots of possibilities, and I'm not asking for the answers, just an acknowledgement that the Foundation would ask the questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep in mind. Memes are ideas. To understand a meme is to carry/be-infected-with said meme. You might not react the same way, but you are a carrier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In the Foundationverse I think anyone who DIDN'T have this response to space travel would be the crazy one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
=\ I don't particularly like taking real-life tragedies like the Challenger disaster and trying to explain them as SCP-related nm

Otherwise, this seems fine, but I feel it needs more fleshing out with regards to its effects on people (as in, why Alpha, Beta, or Gamma distinctions, any pattern or lack of pattern in it) or what it even is to begin with, or what it's not, if anything.

As it stands now, I don't see enough evidence to even consider that this SCP even exists, or couldn't be attributed to some other sort of mischievous being or effect related to some other SCP (I'm reminded of the 3D glasses one, which reveals to the wearer sentient beings apparently hidden all around the world that attack people who address them)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like this better without the "It was memetic the whole time!" angle. Neutralvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dad, are you space?
Yes, son.
Now stay in your room, where it's safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rather like this one. Specifically, I like it being so close after the decommissioning of the US space program; that just kind of resonated with me. Also some of the above mentioned reasons (space) make it work as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was some stellar writing.

No but really, this was very well written. I liked it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I reread. While your additional documentation adds a little more depth to the work, I think that systemically this one is just not my thing. That being said, I really appreciate your willingness to address criticism and make adjustments.

You're in the positives, so I wouldn't worry too much anyway. I look forward to seeing your contributions in future.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be honest, I didn't even think about that interpretation when I wrote this. To me, it was just one of many possible explanations for critics of space travel. I didn't mean for it to come across like that at all; do you think a quick explanation of that could be snuck into a paragraph at some point? I'd hate to lose marks for something that shouldn't even be in there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for all the feedback! (lol forgot to hit 'reply' and needed filler)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's just so tawdry; "here's three elaborate explanations for why people who don't agree with manned space flight are the crazy"…once you figure out the gag in case alpha, there's two more cases that are essentially the same. Where's the build up and the release? Dramatic writing needs changes in level. This stays on exactly one plane throughout; and it's not a great plane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
People keep saying 'the universe' here.

Actually, see, that's the thing. It's not the universe. Or the cosmos. Not the stuff within space, not space dust or Lovecraftian monsters or nebulas or anything else, bigger or smaller, but just space. (Near as I can tell, anyway.)

For some reason the fact that it's specifically space is what really makes it work for me on a conceptual level. If it were nearly anything else, the concept would fail for me.

Also, a side note:

For starters, I'm not a fan of people being skeptical of space travel attributed to an anomalous phenomenon.

I strongly agree with this. Not because it (to me) implies making fun of people with legit criticisms — but because I really dislike real-life psychological or cultural phenomena attributed to "an SCP did it lol". It usually is a lesser explanation than the actual more fascinating reality. In very rare cases this can work, but I don't think it's working here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Alright, I tried, but it seems way too heavy-handed. Subtlety is not my strong suit. I have no idea how well this works as a "kicker" or even merely as an elaboration. I could try and incorporate suggestions of (memeticality? memeticism? memeness?) into the letter, if the email is a bit too ham-fisted. Please let me know if this works or not! Thanks for the constructive feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll try and fix them the best I can, especially the space shuttle coverup

That's the effort common to successful writers on the wiki. I'll be sure to check back in and reevaluate in light of any changes you make.

Do you think elaboration on this would help resolve that issue?

Possibly. In my own view, I think the "there's a legitimate reason behind all this" twist is a viable direction to go in. I could actually get behind the notion of the researcher coming to the conclusion that the carriers are right and doing something about it without being infected more than I think I could get behind "alternate memetic transmission".

I can't guarantee that it'll work, but I will say that I think there's room for you to expand the researcher's diary in order to better sell the plot point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A couple quick responses before I have to go: 1) all of this makes sense, and I'm really mad that there are so many glaring errors. I'll try and fix them the best I can, especially the space shuttle coverup. b) the point of the last bit with the researcher instantly believing the carrier is that there is some memetic element to 2000, but the Foundation hasn't found it yet. Do you think elaboration on this would help resolve that issue? Thanks for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There are too many conceptual issues with this one. For starters, I'm not a fan of people being skeptical of space travel attributed to an anomalous phenomenon. It feels too much like a "take that" to people critical of manned space exploration, and there are legitimate arguments to be made depending on the mission involved and the context. As for the "we faked the moon landing" people being under the effect of a skip, never attribute to anomalies what can be explained by stupidity.

As for the SCP spaceship blowing up, I dunno. I mean, I'm glad that the obvious "ship was publicly identified as Challenger/Columbia" cover story wasn't used, but I'm also not buying that a manned spacecraft blowing up didn't represent a massive publicity issue for the Foundation. Someone more knowledgable about aerospace issues can correct me if I'm wrong here, but I cannot imagine that it's possible to send something the size of a space shuttle up into the atmosphere at escape velocity without someone noticing, much less if it explodes. I wouldn't necessarily want to see a novel on it or anything, but no mention whatsoever of the threat of exposure from the incident seems weird.

And the last note. The researcher seems waaaay too easily convinced here. "So, says here you're under the effects of an anomalous mental thing. But the insane stuff you're telling me sounds legit, so I'll go blow up this spaceship. KTHXBYE." There's nothing in that narrative that suggests that the alpha carrier should be believed, and it feels like you're relying on [DATA EXPUNGED] to do the heavy lifting that should be occurring in the narrative in order to sell the reader on the plot twist.

Finally, there is absolutely no way I'm going to buy the "benevolent cosmos" stuff when said cosmos is presumably also responsible for the horrible mind-breaking monsters that it's trying to protect us from. And why would space travel even matter in that case? We're somehow undetectable to the eldritch horrors if we don't leave the Earth? There's not a lot of internal consistency here.

There's potential in the "abnormal brainwave beamed at us from elsewhere for mysterious reasons" concept here, but as it's presented right now there's just too much in the way of systemic issues for me to refrain from downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say almost exactly the opposite here. I think the execution is good, but the idea that 'the universe' is alive, sapient, and benevolent just completely destroys any suspension of disbelief. I was enjoying the article, especially the collapsible close phrases, but the ending just left me cold. Sorry.

I'm not voting until the end of the contest, but I can't really think of a way for you to improve this for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't really works for me.. I just don't like the idea of the universe being kind of sentient, and trying to protect us. It just doesn't seem logic to me. The whole thing with the "what if we are in fact right" was good, I have to admit it, but it just don't like it…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this concept. I'm unsure about the execution. I'm kind of bleh that … the entity [w/o spoiling] affects people in such a disappointingly straightforward way. But I really like the nature & motivations of the entity.

So… No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since we've apparently developed counter-memetic security precautions, it's probably a good idea to mention ongoing research.

Overall, I liked this one. Didn't love it. It's a solid skip, it had a story, I could actually follow the story, and it doesn't preclude the existence of other skips in space. That's of course assuming anything Redacted Dead Guy said was true.

+1 based on overall quality. I don't know if it deserves to win, but it definitely deserves to make it through the contest with positive numbers.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Technically, I'm not 'guy' but, anyway… you explicitly spell out the conceit in the document.
That is…
…Doubleplusungood.

Also, the science is kinda ungood (brainwaves? You mean, like, ECG bands? OR neural oscilations? Something else mayhaps?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm glad you like it! Do you think I should add a footnote that reads something along the lines of "research into possible memetic qualities of SCP-2000 is still ongoing"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it!
Especially the bit at the end. It makes me wonder if the affected alpha was influenced by her speech, or if SCP-2000 was spread by this interaction.
